annotations.label: Transitivity tacitly required

anchor.snippet: tacitly require the homogeneous relation

annotations.note: This is a meta-convention, not a theorem; though Mathlib's `IsWellOrder` does derive an `IsTrans` instance automatically.

annotations.label: Well-order

anchor.snippet: a total ordering on S with the property that every non-empty subset of S has a least element

annotations.note: `IsWellOrder` is a relation that is well-founded and trichotomous (hence a well-founded linear order).

annotations.label: Well-ordered set

anchor.snippet: together with the ordering is then called a well-ordered set

annotations.note: `WellOrder` bundles a type, a relation, and a proof that the relation is an `IsWellOrder`.

annotations.label: Existence of least element

anchor.snippet: Every non-empty well-ordered set has a least element

annotations.note: `WellFounded.has_min` gives a minimal element of any nonempty set under a well-founded relation, generalizing the well-order case.

annotations.label: Unique successor

anchor.snippet: has a unique successor (next element)

annotations.note: A well-order is made into a `SuccOrder` whose `Order.succ` is the next element (the min of strictly larger elements).

annotations.label: Least upper bound of bounded subsets

anchor.snippet: contains for every subset T with an upper bound a least upper bound

annotations.note: Any well-founded linear order with a bottom is conditionally complete, so every bounded-above set has a least upper bound.

annotations.label: Non-strict yields strict well ordering

anchor.snippet: is a non-strict well ordering, then

annotations.note: From a `LinearOrder` with `WellFoundedLT` Mathlib derives `IsWellOrder α (· < ·)`, i.e. the strict version is a strict well order.

annotations.label: Strict well ordering characterization

anchor.snippet: is a strict well ordering if and only if it is a well-founded strict total order

annotations.note: `IsWellOrder` is defined as well-founded + trichotomous and carries an `IsStrictTotalOrder` instance, which is exactly a well-founded strict total order.

annotations.label: Order type ordinal isomorphism

anchor.snippet: is uniquely order isomorphic to a unique ordinal number , called the order type

annotations.note: `Ordinal.type` assigns the order type, and `Ordinal.type_eq` shows two well orders are order-isomorphic iff they have the same ordinal.

annotations.label: Well-ordering theorem

anchor.snippet: The well-ordering theorem , which is equivalent to the axiom of choice

annotations.note: `exists_wellFoundedLT` (Zermelo's theorem) gives every type a well-order using choice; the explicit equivalence to AC is not separately formalized.

annotations.label: Transfinite induction applicability

anchor.snippet: the proof technique of transfinite induction can be used to prove

annotations.note: `WellFoundedLT.induction` (and `Ordinal.inductionOn`) provide transfinite/well-founded induction.

annotations.label: Well-ordering principle

anchor.snippet: is commonly called the well-ordering principle

annotations.note: `type_nat_lt` (typeLT ℕ = ω) records that ℕ under `<` is a well order, the content of the well-ordering principle.

annotations.label: Standard order on naturals

anchor.snippet: The standard ordering ≤ of the natural numbers is a well ordering

annotations.note: `type_nat_lt` states ℕ's standard order is a well order of type ω (ℕ also has a `WellFoundedLT` instance).

annotations.label: Evens-before-odds order (type ω+ω)

anchor.snippet: all even numbers are less than all odd numbers

annotations.note: This specific reordering of ℕ giving order type ω+ω is not formalized.

annotations.label: Standard order on integers not a well order

anchor.snippet: the standard ordering ≤ of the integers is not a well ordering

annotations.note: There is no explicit statement that ℤ is not well-ordered (ℤ simply has no `WellFoundedLT` instance and is `NoMinOrder`).

annotations.label: Well order R of integers (type ω+ω)

anchor.snippet: The following binary relation R is an example of well ordering of the integers

annotations.note: This explicit well order of ℤ of type ω+ω is not formalized.

annotations.label: Alternate well order of integers (type ω)

anchor.snippet: Another relation for well ordering the integers is the following definition

annotations.note: This alternate well order of ℤ of type ω is not formalized.

annotations.label: Real interval not a well order

anchor.snippet: The standard ordering ≤ of any real interval is not a well ordering

annotations.note: No explicit statement that real intervals are not well-ordered by ≤.

annotations.label: ZFC implies a well order of the reals

anchor.snippet: one can show that there is a well order of the reals

annotations.note: Instantiating `exists_wellFoundedLT` at ℝ yields a well order of the reals.

annotations.label: Sierpiński: ZF+GCH implies AC

anchor.snippet: Wacław Sierpiński proved that ZF + GCH

annotations.note: Sierpiński's theorem that GCH implies AC over ZF is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: No definable well order from ZFC+GCH alone

anchor.snippet: are not sufficient to prove the existence of a definable

annotations.note: This independence/non-definability metatheorem is not formalized.

annotations.label: ZFC+V=L gives definable well order

anchor.snippet: it follows from ZFC+V=L that a particular formula well orders the reals

annotations.note: V=L and the definable well order of the reals it provides are not formalized.

annotations.label: Uncountable real subset cannot be well ordered by ≤

anchor.snippet: An uncountable subset of the real numbers with the standard ordering ≤ cannot be a well order

annotations.note: No statement that an uncountable subset of ℝ cannot be well-ordered by the standard ≤ was found.

annotations.label: Naturals well ordered by ≤

anchor.snippet: The natural numbers are a well order under the standard ordering ≤

annotations.note: ℕ under its standard order is a well order (type ω), via `type_nat_lt` and the `WellFoundedLT ℕ` instance.

annotations.label: Set with no least element

anchor.snippet: has no least element and is therefore not a well order under standard ordering ≤

annotations.note: The specific example {1/n} lacking a least element is not formalized.

annotations.label: Real set of order type ω

anchor.snippet: Examples of well orders

annotations.note: This concrete subset of ℝ of order type ω is not formalized.

annotations.label: Real set of order type ω²

anchor.snippet: has order type ω 2

annotations.note: This concrete subset of ℝ of order type ω² is not formalized.

annotations.label: Real set of order type ω+1

anchor.snippet: has order type ω + 1

annotations.note: This concrete subset of ℝ of order type ω+1 is not formalized.

annotations.label: Equivalent formulations of well order

anchor.snippet: If a set is totally ordered , then the following are equivalent to each other

annotations.note: Individual equivalences exist (`wellFounded_iff_has_min`, `wellFounded_iff_isEmpty_descending_chain`) but not bundled as one TFAE statement.

annotations.label: Initial segment

anchor.snippet: An initial segment , determined by an element

annotations.note: `PrincipalSeg.ofElement r a` is the initial segment determined by `a`, with range `Set.Iio a`.

annotations.label: Initial segments are lower sets

anchor.snippet: an initial segment is the same thing as a lower set in order theory

annotations.note: `InitialSeg.isLowerSet_range` shows the range of an initial-segment embedding is an `IsLowerSet`.

annotations.label: Ordinal number via initial segments

anchor.snippet: an ordinal number is a well-ordered set

annotations.note: `ZFSet.IsOrdinal` defines von Neumann ordinals as transitive sets well-ordered by ∈, where each element is its own initial segment.

annotations.label: Not isomorphic to proper initial segment

anchor.snippet: A well-ordered set is never isomorphic to a proper initial segment of itself

annotations.note: `PrincipalSeg.irrefl` shows there is no principal-segment embedding `r ≺i r` of a well order into itself.

annotations.label: Comparability of well-ordered sets

anchor.snippet: given two well-ordered sets

annotations.note: `InitialSeg.total` shows of two well orders, one embeds as an initial segment of the other.

annotations.label: Morphisms preserve initial segments

anchor.snippet: A morphism between well-ordered sets sends initial segments to initial segments

annotations.note: `InitialSeg.image_Iio` shows an initial-segment morphism maps `Set.Iio a` to `Set.Iio (f a)`.

annotations.label: Ordering on the class of well-ordered sets

anchor.snippet: Initial segments give an ordering

annotations.note: `Ordinal` is the class of well orders up to isomorphism carrying a `LinearOrder` defined via initial-segment embeddings.

annotations.label: Union of a chain is well-ordered

anchor.snippet: The union of a chain of well-ordered sets is then well-ordered

annotations.note: No direct statement that the union of a chain of well-ordered sets (each an initial segment of the next) is well-ordered was found.

annotations.label: Initial-segment order coincides with inclusion for ordinals

anchor.snippet: this ordering given by initial segments coincides with set inclusion

annotations.note: The von Neumann ordinal development (∈/⊆ order via `ZFSet.IsOrdinal`) exists, but the exact coincidence statement isn't isolated.

annotations.label: Well-foundedness via initial segments

anchor.snippet: is well-founded if and only if it is covered by well-founded initial segments

annotations.note: `wellFounded_iff_principalSeg` characterizes well-foundedness via well-foundedness of principal segments below, closely related to this statement.

annotations.label: Order topology on a well-ordered set

anchor.snippet: Every well-ordered set can be made into a topological space by endowing it with the order topology

annotations.note: `Preorder.topology` / `OrderTopology` provide the order topology for any (pre)order, hence for well orders.

annotations.label: Isolated points and limit points

anchor.snippet: there can be two kinds of elements: isolated points

annotations.note: `Order.IsSuccLimit` captures limit elements (vs successors), but the topological isolated/limit-point dichotomy for well orders isn't separately stated.

annotations.label: Cofinal subset

anchor.snippet: A subset is cofinal in the whole set if and only if it is unbounded in the whole set

annotations.note: `IsCofinal` defines a cofinal set in any ordered type as one with elements ≥ every element (i.e. unbounded).

annotations.label: First-countability criterion

anchor.snippet: is a first-countable space if and only if it has order type less than or equal to ω 1

annotations.note: The criterion that an ordinal-indexed order topology is first-countable iff its type is ≤ ω₁ was not found.

annotations.label: Order type as unique ordinal

anchor.snippet: called the order type of the well-ordered set

annotations.note: `Ordinal.type` is the unique ordinal (order type) attached to a well order.

annotations.label: Finite set size equals order type

anchor.snippet: of a finite set is equal to the order type

annotations.note: `Ordinal.type_fintype` shows the order type of a finite well order equals its `Fintype.card`.

annotations.label: Order type determines cardinality

anchor.snippet: the order type determines the cardinality , but not conversely

annotations.note: `Ordinal.card` (with `card_type`) is the well-defined map from order type to cardinality; the 'not conversely' direction is not a single theorem.

annotations.label: Uncountably many order types for countable sets

anchor.snippet: the set of possible order types is uncountable

annotations.note: The infrastructure of ω₁ and ℵ₁ > ℵ₀ exists, but the specific statement that the countable order types form an uncountable set isn't isolated.
